title: Discover the origins and evolution of PL/I and CICS.
description: Learn how PL/I and CICS shaped modern mainframe programming and transaction processing.

theory.markdown:
## PL/I history: why it was created

### A unification goal in early IBM computing
PL/I was conceived in an era where scientific and commercial programming had different dominant languages and practices. One of PL/I’s core ambitions was to provide a single language that could support a broad range of programming tasks.

### What to take away as a learner
- PL/I’s feature set reflects its ambition: rich data descriptions, control structures, and facilities intended for varied domains.
- The historical context matters because it explains *why* PL/I looks the way it does (and why it persists in enterprise environments).

### Media
If you add the course images to `public/images/`, you can reference them like:
- `![](/images/360.jpg)`

---

### References (academic/official)
1. George Radin, *The early history and characteristics of PL/I*, ACM SIGPLAN Notices (1978). `https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/960118.808389`
theory.content:
Welcome to PL/I History

PL/I, short for Programming Language One, was developed by IBM in the early 1960s during a period when computing was becoming increasingly important for scientific institutions, engineering environments, large businesses, and government organizations. At the time, no single programming language fully satisfied the needs of all these domains. Scientific users often preferred FORTRAN because of its numerical and mathematical strengths, while business organizations relied heavily on COBOL for file processing, reporting, and commercial data handling. IBM wanted to bridge this divide and create a language that could unify these different programming worlds into one more powerful and flexible solution.

That vision led to the creation of PL/I as part of IBM's broader System/360 strategy. Introduced officially in 1964, PL/I was not intended to be just another language. It was designed as an ambitious attempt to support business logic, scientific computation, structured data, systems programming, and complex control flow within one language specification. This made PL/I one of the most advanced and wide-ranging programming languages of its era.

Why PL/I Was Important

PL/I stood out because it introduced and combined features that many languages at the time either lacked entirely or supported only partially. It offered a rich set of data types, support for decimal arithmetic, string manipulation, array processing, recursion, multitasking, and exception handling. This combination made PL/I especially attractive for organizations that needed a language capable of handling both numerical processing and administrative data processing in the same application environment.

In practice, this meant that PL/I was capable of supporting complicated enterprise software in a way that reflected the growing complexity of real-world computing. Instead of forcing teams to choose between a language optimized for science and one optimized for business, PL/I attempted to offer both. That made it especially relevant on IBM mainframes, where different application needs often existed side by side.

Technical Evolution

As computing matured through the 1970s and 1980s, PL/I continued to evolve. IBM improved the language and its compiler technology to support more structured programming practices, stronger development tooling, and better integration with large enterprise operating environments. Features such as condition handling through ON units, improved I/O control, and support for modular program organization gave PL/I a reputation for technical sophistication and reliability.

PL/I also became deeply connected to the IBM mainframe ecosystem. It was used in environments where stability, throughput, and maintainability mattered more than trend-driven language adoption. For enterprise teams, PL/I was not merely a historical experiment. It became a practical tool for building real systems that processed large volumes of business and institutional data reliably over long periods of time.

Why PL/I Still Matters Today

Although newer languages such as Java, Python, and C# dominate much of modern software development, PL/I still remains relevant in environments where long-term stability and continuity are essential. Many large organizations, especially in finance, insurance, public administration, and critical infrastructure, continue to rely on applications originally written in PL/I. These applications often represent decades of business knowledge and refinement, which makes them expensive and risky to replace entirely.

As a result, PL/I remains part of modernization efforts on the mainframe. Instead of disappearing, it often continues to exist alongside newer technologies. Organizations may expose PL/I-based logic through APIs, integrate it with modern data services, or maintain it as part of a hybrid enterprise architecture. This means PL/I is not only historically important, but also operationally relevant in systems that still deliver value today.

PL/I's Legacy

The legacy of PL/I lies in both its ambition and its influence. It demonstrated that programming languages could aim far beyond narrow use cases and could support multiple domains within a single coherent design. It also helped establish expectations around advanced language features that later became common in other environments, including robust exception handling, richer data manipulation, and more flexible application structure.

Studying PL/I helps learners understand not only one language, but also an important moment in the history of software engineering. It shows how language design responded to the realities of enterprise computing and how those early design decisions still echo in today's systems. For anyone learning about mainframes, enterprise software, or the evolution of programming languages, PL/I remains an important chapter in computing history.
keyConcepts: Development of PL/I within IBM's System/360 initiative
Combination of FORTRAN-style computation and COBOL-style business processing
Long-term relevance of PL/I in enterprise and mainframe environments

questions.question: In what year was PL/I officially introduced?
Which two languages most strongly influenced PL/I's design?

theory.content:
Welcome to CICS History

CICS, which stands for Customer Information Control System, emerged during a time when interactive business computing on IBM mainframes was becoming increasingly complex. In the late 1960s, organizations needed better ways to process high volumes of business transactions while serving many users and terminals at the same time. The raw operating-system and telecommunications interfaces available then were powerful, but they required significant low-level programming knowledge. Application developers had to manage communication details, task control, data access, and recovery logic in ways that were difficult, repetitive, and error-prone.

CICS was created to simplify this environment by offering a shared transaction-processing framework on top of IBM mainframe systems. Instead of forcing every team to solve terminal handling, file control, task scheduling, and failure recovery from scratch, CICS provided a standard execution environment in which these services were already available. This made it easier to build reliable business applications and helped establish a common architectural model for enterprise transaction processing.

Origins and Early Growth

The origins of CICS go back to IBM's efforts to support large-scale customer information systems and transaction-heavy operational workloads. It was first introduced in the late 1960s, initially as a practical response to real business demands rather than as a purely theoretical software project. Organizations needed systems that could respond quickly to requests, maintain consistent business records, and continue functioning even when workloads increased. CICS met this need by organizing application execution around transactions, which became one of the defining patterns of enterprise mainframe computing.

In its earlier forms, CICS was closely tied to assembler-style development models and relatively low-level programming techniques. Over time, however, it evolved into a more structured and more accessible platform. Developers could increasingly use higher-level languages and command-level interfaces instead of relying entirely on lower-level constructs. This broadening of development options helped CICS expand far beyond its original niche and become a standard platform for a wide range of business applications.

Why CICS Became So Important

CICS became central to enterprise computing because it addressed some of the most difficult operational problems in large organizations. Businesses did not just need applications that worked once; they needed systems that worked continuously, safely, and at scale. A banking transaction, insurance update, reservation lookup, inventory check, or government record change had to be handled quickly and accurately, often with many users connected at once. CICS offered a runtime environment built specifically for that kind of work.

Its strength came from combining several essential enterprise concerns into one platform: transaction control, communication handling, concurrency support, recoverability, and integration with other core IBM technologies. CICS allowed developers to focus more on business logic and less on plumbing. This was one of the biggest reasons for its success. It reduced repeated engineering effort across teams while increasing consistency across applications.

Evolution Through Enterprise Computing

As enterprise systems grew larger and more interconnected, CICS evolved with them. During the 1970s, 1980s, and beyond, it gained stronger management features, better recovery controls, broader support for multiple application styles, and tighter integration with IBM mainframe operating environments. This helped CICS remain useful even as hardware, organizational expectations, and development methods changed.

CICS also adapted to changing business expectations around availability and reliability. Large enterprises needed software that could survive failures, restart safely, protect data integrity, and continue to process requests under pressure. CICS became trusted precisely because it was built around those requirements. Over time, it developed a reputation not just as a transaction monitor, but as a proven enterprise application platform.

Why People Still Use CICS Today

CICS remains relevant today because many organizations still depend on systems that perform mission-critical business work every day. These systems are often not simple old programs that can be discarded without consequence. They contain deeply embedded business rules, operational processes, and institutional knowledge developed over decades. Replacing them fully would involve high cost, major risk, and long transition periods. In many cases, keeping them and modernizing around them is the more practical and responsible choice.

That is where CICS continues to provide value. It allows organizations to preserve dependable core transaction logic while extending it into newer architectures. A company can continue running established COBOL, PL/I, or assembler applications under CICS while exposing selected functions to APIs, web applications, service layers, mobile channels, or broader modernization frameworks. This means CICS supports continuity without preventing change.

Another reason people still use CICS is performance and scalability. Enterprise transaction workloads often require extremely fast response times, dependable throughput, and strong operational discipline. CICS has a long history of meeting those needs in sectors such as banking, insurance, retail, transportation, and government. For organizations where transaction integrity and continuous availability matter more than hype, CICS remains a logical platform choice.

Modern Relevance and Integration

One of the biggest misconceptions about CICS is that it belongs only to the past. In reality, modern CICS environments are part of ongoing enterprise modernization strategies. Rather than freezing old workloads in place, organizations use CICS to connect proven systems to modern development models, management tools, and integration layers. In that sense, CICS often functions as a bridge between traditional mainframe strengths and modern software delivery expectations.

This modern relevance is tied to the fact that CICS continues to evolve as part of IBM's enterprise software stack. It supports mixed-language development, integration with modern tooling, and improved observability and operational capabilities. That makes it possible for organizations to maintain core workloads while also adopting newer engineering practices. Instead of forcing an all-or-nothing choice between legacy and modernity, CICS supports gradual transformation.

For learners, studying CICS provides insight into how enterprise systems are really built and maintained. It shows that software history is not just about obsolete technology, but about platforms that solved hard operational problems so effectively that they continue to matter decades later. Understanding CICS helps explain why transaction processing remains one of the central concerns of enterprise computing and why mainframe platforms still play an active role in today's digital infrastructure.

CICS as Part of Computing History

Historically, CICS is important because it represents a shift from isolated batch-oriented computing toward responsive, controlled, transactional business computing. It helped define how organizations thought about online systems, concurrent users, shared resources, and protected data operations. Its long survival is not accidental. It reflects the fact that CICS addressed enduring enterprise requirements in a disciplined and scalable way.

Even today, CICS remains a symbol of how foundational infrastructure can persist when it continues to deliver business value. It is both a historical milestone and a living platform. That combination makes it one of the most important technologies to study when exploring the history, architecture, and continuing relevance of IBM mainframe computing.
keyConcepts: Origins of CICS as a response to low-level transaction and communication complexity on IBM mainframes
Evolution of CICS from early transaction control software into a mature enterprise platform
Ongoing relevance of CICS for reliability, scalability, modernization, and mission-critical processing

questions.question: Why was CICS originally created?
options: To replace all programming languages on IBM systems
To simplify low-level transaction and communication handling for application programmers
To serve only as a graphics environment
To function as a desktop operating system

questions.question: Why do many organizations still use CICS today?
options: Because it is mainly used for gaming applications
Because it is required for personal laptops
Because it provides a reliable way to run and modernize mission-critical transaction systems
Because it has replaced all cloud platforms
